headline: Audio comparison complete.
dek: The two files are compared as A versus B cortical response estimates. Use the track deltas and brain view to inspect where the difference is concentrated.

a.label: A
a.name: calm_read.wav
a.summary: Steadier pacing, lower attention contrast, and a softer close.

transcript.text: Most teams know their message is not landing.
They test clicks, but they rarely test what the brain is actually doing.
BrainDiff compares two versions and shows the cortical response.
So you can choose the message people remember.

b.label: B
b.name: intimate_read.wav
b.summary: Higher attention and memory contrast through the middle and final windows.

transcript.text: But the brain may be ignoring the part you care about.
BrainDiff shows where each version pulls attention, trust, and memory.
Then it tells you which version deserves to ship.

moments.title: Early attention shift
moments.detail: B is higher on the attention track in the first comparison window.
moments.move: Treat this as a cortical contrast signal, not a quality score.

moments.title: Midpoint trust shift
moments.detail: B is higher on the trust track around the main-claim window.
moments.move: Treat this as a cortical contrast signal, not a quality score.

moments.title: Closing memory shift
moments.detail: B is higher on the memory track near the final window.
moments.move: Treat this as a cortical contrast signal, not a quality score.

recommendations: Largest positive shift: B attention and memory.
Largest negative shift: B has lower listening effort than A.
Review the cortical surface and transcript windows before selecting a version.
